questions.question:
Consider the circuit in below figure which has a four bit binary number b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 as input and a five bit binary number, d_4 d_3 d_2 d_1 d_0 as output. The circuit uses a 4-bit binary adder where the second input is fixed at 0110 (i.e., 6) and carry-in is 0, producing a 5-bit output (carry-out is d_4).

A. Binary to Hex conversion
B. Binary to BCD conversion
C. Binary to Gray code conversion
D. Binary to radix-12 conversion
questions.to_find: What conversion does the circuit (4-bit binary adder adding 6 to the input) implement?

understand.plain: A 4-bit binary number is fed into a 4-bit adder. The second input to the adder is hardwired to 0110 (decimal 6), and carry-in is 0. The 5-bit output (including carry-out) is the result. Adding 6 to a binary number is the standard correction step used when converting binary to BCD (Binary Coded Decimal) — specifically, if a BCD digit exceeds 9, you add 6 to skip the invalid hex states A-F.

keywords.explain: A number encoding where each decimal digit (0-9) is represented by exactly 4 binary bits. Values 1010-1111 (10-15) are invalid in BCD.
keywords.example: Decimal 9 = 1001 in BCD; decimal 10 = 0001 0000 (two BCD digits)

keywords.explain: When two BCD digits are added and the sum exceeds 9 (or a carry occurs), adding 6 to the sum corrects it to valid BCD form. This is because there are 6 invalid states (A-F) between 9 and the next BCD decade.
keywords.example: BCD 9 + BCD 4 = binary 13 (1101), which is invalid BCD; 1101 + 0110 = 1 0011, giving BCD digits 1 and 3, i.e., decimal 13.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="420" height="180" viewBox="0 0 420 180"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="60" width="400" height="70" class="bg" rx="8"/><rect x="130" y="70" width="160" height="50" class="hi" rx="6"/><text x="210" y="102" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">4-bit Binary Adder</text><text x="60" y="85" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">Input: b_3b_2b_1b_0</text><text x="60" y="105" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">(0 to 15)</text><text x="360" y="85" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">Output: d_4d_3d_2d_1d_0</text><text x="360" y="105" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">(5-bit)</text><text x="210" y="155" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">Second input fixed = 0110 (6), C_in = 0</text><text x="210" y="35" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">Adds 6 to binary input → BCD correction</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 4-bit binary adder takes b3b2b1b0 as one input and has 0110 (=6) hardwired as the second input with carry-in=0. The 5-bit output d4d3d2d1d0 is the sum.

given.aim: Determine what digital conversion is performed by adding 6 to a 4-bit binary number and producing a 5-bit output.

terms.term: 4-bit binary input b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0
terms.meaning: The input binary number ranging from 0000 to 1111 (0 to 15).
terms.connects: This is the binary value to be converted.

terms.meaning: The adder always adds decimal 6 to the input.
terms.example: If input = 9 (1001), output = 9+6 = 15 = 01111, but with BCD correction it gives 0001 0101 = 15 in BCD.
terms.connects: Adding 6 is the standard BCD correction step.

terms.term: 5-bit output d_4 d_3 d_2 d_1 d_0
terms.meaning: The sum including the carry-out bit d_4 as the most significant bit.
terms.connects: The 5-bit output represents the BCD-corrected value.

terms.meaning: If the sum of two BCD digits exceeds 9 or a carry is generated, add 6 to correct to valid BCD.
terms.connects: This is exactly what the circuit does.

given.plan: Recognize that adding 6 (0110) to a 4-bit binary input and taking the 5-bit result is the BCD correction operation. When a binary sum exceeds 9, adding 6 skips the six invalid BCD code points (1010 to 1111) and wraps to the correct BCD representation. This identifies the circuit as performing Binary to BCD conversion.

steps.title: Identify the adder configuration
steps.apply: The circuit is a 4-bit adder with one input = b_3b_2b_1b_0 and the other hardwired to 0110 (decimal 6), carry-in = 0. The 5-bit output is d_4d_3d_2d_1d_0 where d_4 is the carry-out.
steps.note: The fixed addition of 6 is the key clue to the conversion type.

steps.title: Relate to BCD conversion
steps.formula_raw: BCD correction: if sum > 9 or carry, add 6
steps.apply:
In BCD arithmetic, after adding two BCD digits, if the result > 9 (or carry = 1), we add 6 to correct it. This circuit always adds 6 — it converts binary to BCD by applying the excess-6 correction. For inputs 0-9, the output still needs the upper nibble check; for 10-15, adding 6 correctly creates the two-digit BCD representation via the carry-out d_4.

Examples:
- Input = 0 (0000): 0+6=6 (00110) — but for input < 4, output is in single-digit range after adjustment
- Input = 9 (1001): 9+6=15 (01111), d_4=0
- Input = 10 (1010): 10+6=16 (10000), d_4=1, lower nibble = 0000 → BCD = 0001 0000 = 16
- Input = 15 (1111): 15+6=21 (10101), d_4=1, lower = 0101 → BCD = 0010 0001 = 21

This matches BCD representation of the original binary values, confirming Binary to BCD conversion.
steps.note: Adding 6 shifts the binary count to skip over the 6 invalid BCD codes (A-F), wrapping correctly into the next BCD decade via the carry.

steps.title: Eliminate other options
steps.apply:
Binary to Hex: trivial, no conversion needed since binary IS hex already.
Binary to Gray code: Gray code uses XOR of adjacent bits, not addition of 6.
Binary to radix-12: adding 6 does not produce base-12 encoding.
Only Binary to BCD conversion uses the +6 correction step.
steps.note: BCD conversion is the only one that requires adding 6.

solution.result: The circuit implements Binary to BCD conversion. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: BCD addition correction rule: if binary sum exceeds 9 or carry is generated, add 6 (0110) to get valid BCD output.

questions.question:
Consider the circuit in figure. f implements

The circuit is a 4-to-1 multiplexer with select lines S_1 = A and S_0 = B. The inputs are:
- I_0 (selected when AB = 00): C
- I_1 (selected when AB = 01): C' (complement of C)
- I_2 (selected when AB = 10): C' (complement of C)
- I_3 (selected when AB = 11): C

A. A'BC + A'BC' + ABC
B. A + B + C
C. A XOR B XOR C
D. AB + BC + CA
questions.answer: C. A XOR B XOR C
questions.to_find: The Boolean function f implemented by the 4-to-1 MUX circuit.

understand.plain: A 4-to-1 multiplexer selects one of 4 inputs based on 2 select lines. Here, A and B are the select lines (A=S1, B=S0). Depending on the value of AB, the output f equals either C or C' (complement of C). By tracing all four combinations of AB and the corresponding input (C or C'), we can determine the Boolean function.

keywords.explain: A digital selector with 4 data inputs, 2 select lines, and 1 output. The output equals the data input whose index matches the binary value of the select lines.
XOR outputs 1 when an odd number of inputs are 1. For two bits: A XOR B = 1 iff A != B. For three bits: A XOR B XOR C = 1 iff an odd number of A, B, C are 1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="380" height="230" viewBox="0 0 380 230"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="150" y="30" width="110" height="140" class="hi" rx="6"/><text x="205" y="55" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">4-to-1</text><text x="205" y="78" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">MUX</text><text x="90" y="72" class="sub ink" text-anchor="end">C (I_0)</text><text x="90" y="97" class="sub ink" text-anchor="end">C' (I_1)</text><text x="90" y="122" class="sub ink" text-anchor="end">C' (I_2)</text><text x="90" y="147" class="sub ink" text-anchor="end">C (I_3)</text><line x1="95" y1="68" x2="150" y2="68" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="95" y1="93" x2="150" y2="93" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="95" y1="118" x2="150" y2="118" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="95" y1="143" x2="150" y2="143" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="260" y1="100" x2="310" y2="100" stroke="#1D9E75" stroke-width="2"/><text x="315" y="104" class="sub ink">f</text><text x="175" y="190" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">S_1=A</text><text x="235" y="190" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">S_0=B</text><line x1="180" y1="170" x2="180" y2="200" stroke="#2C2C2A" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="230" y1="170" x2="230" y2="200" stroke="#2C2C2A" stroke-width="2"/><text x="205" y="220" class="tag mut" text-anchor="middle">Select: A (MSB), B (LSB)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 4-to-1 MUX with inputs I0=C, I1=C', I2=C', I3=C; select lines S1=A (MSB), S0=B (LSB); output f.

given.aim: Find the Boolean function f output by the 4-to-1 MUX given its input and select line connections.

terms.term: Select lines: S_1 = A, S_0 = B
terms.meaning: A is the most significant select bit, B is the least significant. Together they select one of the 4 inputs.
terms.connects: Determines which input (C or C') is routed to the output.

terms.term: MUX inputs: I_0=C, I_1=C', I_2=C', I_3=C
terms.meaning: When AB=00 or AB=11, f=C. When AB=01 or AB=10, f=C'.
terms.connects: The pattern of C and C' on inputs encodes the function.

terms.meaning: f = 1 when an odd number of A, B, C are 1 — this is the XOR of three variables.
terms.connects: Matches the truth table of A XOR B XOR C.

given.plan: Build the truth table by evaluating f for all 8 combinations of A, B, C using the MUX input rule: f = C when AB = 00 or 11, f = C' when AB = 01 or 10. Then identify which option matches this truth table.

steps.title: Trace all combinations through the MUX
steps.formula_raw: f = A'B'(I_0) + A'B(I_1) + AB'(I_2) + AB(I_3) = A'B'C + A'BC' + AB'C' + ABC
steps.apply:
Substituting the inputs:
f = A'B'C + A'BC' + AB'C' + ABC

Truth table:
A B C | AB-select | Input selected | f
0 0 0 |   00      |  I_0 = C = 0   | 0
0 0 1 |   00      |  I_0 = C = 1   | 1
0 1 0 |   01      |  I_1 = C'= 1   | 1
0 1 1 |   01      |  I_1 = C'= 0   | 0
1 0 0 |   10      |  I_2 = C'= 1   | 1
1 0 1 |   10      |  I_2 = C'= 0   | 0
1 1 0 |   11      |  I_3 = C = 0   | 0
1 1 1 |   11      |  I_3 = C = 1   | 1

f = 1 at minterms: 1, 2, 4, 7 (i.e., ABC combinations with odd number of 1s).
steps.note: f is 1 exactly when an odd number of inputs {A,B,C} are 1.

steps.title: Identify the function
steps.formula_raw: A XOR B XOR C = 1 iff odd number of {A,B,C} = 1
steps.apply:
The truth table for A XOR B XOR C:
- 000: 0; 001: 1; 010: 1; 011: 0; 100: 1; 101: 0; 110: 0; 111: 1
This matches our truth table exactly.

Check options:
A. A'BC + A'BC' + ABC = A'B(C+C') + ABC = A'B + ABC — this is NOT our function (only 3 minterms, and wrong ones).
B. A+B+C = 1 for minterms 1-7 (7 ones) — doesn't match.
C. A XOR B XOR C = minterms 1,2,4,7 — matches exactly.
D. AB+BC+CA — minterms where 2 or 3 variables = 1, i.e., 3,5,6,7 — doesn't match.
steps.note: A XOR B XOR C is the 3-input XOR (parity) function.

solution.result: f = A XOR B XOR C. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: 4-to-1 MUX function expansion: f = sum of (select-term * input). The pattern C, C', C', C on inputs I0-I3 with A=S1, B=S0 implements the 3-input XOR (odd parity) function.

questions.question:
Consider the synchronous sequential circuit in the below figure. The circuit has three D flip-flops (D_1, D_2, D_3) with outputs Q_1, Q_2, Q_3 (and their complements Q_1', Q_2', Q_3'). The first flip-flop's input D_1 is driven by a NAND gate whose inputs are Q_1' and Q_3 (i.e., D_1 = NAND(Q_1', Q_3) = NOT(Q_1' AND Q_3)). D_2 = Q_1 and D_3 = Q_2 (shift register connection). All flip-flops are clocked by the same clock.

Draw a state diagram which is implemented by the circuit. Use the following names for the states corresponding to the values of flip-flops as given below.

Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | State
 0 |  0 |  0 |  S_0
 0 |  0 |  1 |  S_1
 - |  - |  - |   -
 - |  - |  - |   -
 - |  - |  - |   -
 1 |  1 |  1 |  S_7

(The remaining states S_2 through S_6 correspond to Q1Q2Q3 = 010, 011, 100, 101, 110 respectively.)

A. It is a 3-bit binary up counter
B. It is a 3-bit ring counter
C. It generates the sequence S_0 -> S_1 -> S_3 -> S_7 -> S_6 -> S_4 -> S_0
D. It generates the sequence S_0 -> S_1 -> S_2 -> S_4 -> S_0
questions.answer: C. It generates the sequence S_0 -> S_1 -> S_3 -> S_7 -> S_6 -> S_4 -> S_0
questions.to_find: Draw the state diagram of the synchronous sequential circuit with three D flip-flops, and identify the sequence of states.

understand.plain: This circuit has three D flip-flops connected in a shift-register-like fashion where D_2 = Q_1 and D_3 = Q_2, meaning Q_2 and Q_3 each copy the previous stage's value every clock. The input D_1 is derived from a logic gate involving the current state. By computing D_1 for each state and tracing transitions, we can draw the complete state diagram.

keywords.explain: A storage element that captures the value at its D input on the clock edge and holds it as output Q.
keywords.example: If D=1 at the rising clock edge, Q becomes 1 after the clock.

keywords.explain: A directed graph showing all states of a sequential circuit and the transitions between them on each clock cycle.
keywords.example: S0 ->clock-> S1 means the circuit goes from state S0 to S1 on the next clock edge.

keywords.explain: NAND(A, B) = NOT(A AND B) = 1 unless both inputs are 1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="460" height="200" viewBox="0 0 460 200"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="70" width="70" height="60" class="bg" rx="4"/><text x="45" y="97" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">NAND</text><text x="45" y="115" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">(Q1',Q3)</text><rect x="120" y="55" width="70" height="80" class="hi" rx="4"/><text x="155" y="88" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">D_1 FF</text><text x="155" y="108" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">Q_1</text><rect x="240" y="55" width="70" height="80" class="hi" rx="4"/><text x="275" y="88" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">D_2 FF</text><text x="275" y="108" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">Q_2</text><rect x="360" y="55" width="70" height="80" class="hi" rx="4"/><text x="395" y="88" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">D_3 FF</text><text x="395" y="108" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">Q_3</text><line x1="80" y1="100" x2="120" y2="95" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/><line x1="190" y1="95" x2="240" y2="95" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="310" y1="95" x2="360" y2="95" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2"/><text x="215" y="85" class="tag mut" text-anchor="middle">D_2=Q_1</text><text x="335" y="85" class="tag mut" text-anchor="middle">D_3=Q_2</text><text x="230" y="175" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">D_1 = NAND(Q_1', Q_3); shift: D_2=Q_1, D_3=Q_2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three D flip-flops in series. D_1 is fed by NAND(Q1-bar, Q3). D_2 = Q1 (shift), D_3 = Q2 (shift). All clocked together. Q3 feeds back to the NAND gate along with Q1-bar.

given.aim: Trace the state transitions of the sequential circuit to draw its state diagram.

terms.meaning: Next state of Q_1 equals NOT(NOT(Q_1) AND Q_3) = Q_1 OR NOT(Q_3) = Q_1 + Q_3'.
terms.connects: This determines the next value of Q_1.

terms.meaning: Q_2 and Q_3 shift right: next Q_2 = current Q_1, next Q_3 = current Q_2.
terms.connects: These shift connections determine Q_2 and Q_3 in the next state.

terms.term: State naming (Q1 Q2 Q3 = binary = state number)
terms.meaning: State S_k corresponds to Q1Q2Q3 being the 3-bit binary representation of k.

given.plan: For each state, compute D_1 = NAND(Q_1', Q_3) = Q_1 + Q_3' and D_2 = Q_1, D_3 = Q_2. The next state is (D_1, D_2, D_3). Trace from S_0 until a cycle is found. Then check for unreachable states.

steps.title: Compute next-state function
steps.apply:
Next state Q_1+ = D_1 = Q_1 OR (NOT Q_3)
Next state Q_2+ = D_2 = Q_1
Next state Q_3+ = D_3 = Q_2

For all 8 states:
S_0 (000): Q1=0,Q2=0,Q3=0 → D1=0+1=1, D2=0, D3=0 → next = 100 = S_4
S_1 (001): Q1=0,Q2=0,Q3=1 → D1=0+0=0, D2=0, D3=0 → next = 000 = S_0
S_2 (010): Q1=0,Q2=1,Q3=0 → D1=0+1=1, D2=0, D3=1 → next = 101 = S_5
S_3 (011): Q1=0,Q2=1,Q3=1 → D1=0+0=0, D2=0, D3=1 → next = 001 = S_1
S_4 (100): Q1=1,Q2=0,Q3=0 → D1=1+1=1, D2=1, D3=0 → next = 110 = S_6
S_5 (101): Q1=1,Q2=0,Q3=1 → D1=1+0=1, D2=1, D3=0 → next = 110 = S_6
S_6 (110): Q1=1,Q2=1,Q3=0 → D1=1+1=1, D2=1, D3=1 → next = 111 = S_7
S_7 (111): Q1=1,Q2=1,Q3=1 → D1=1+0=1, D2=1, D3=1 → next = 111 = S_7 ... Wait, re-check.

Re-check S_7 (111): Q1=1,Q2=1,Q3=1 → D1=NAND(Q1',Q3)=NAND(0,1)=NOT(0*1)=NOT(0)=1, D2=Q1=1, D3=Q2=1 → next=111=S_7? That creates a self-loop at S_7.

Re-check the NAND gate inputs from the image: The image shows the feedback is from Q_3 (output of D_3 flip-flop) back to the NAND gate along with Q_1' (complement output of D_1 flip-flop).

Actually re-examining: D_1 = NAND(Q_1', Q_3). Let me recheck S_3 and S_7:
S_3 (011): D1=NAND(1,1)=0, D2=0, D3=1 → next=001=S_1. Correct.
S_7 (111): D1=NAND(0,1)=1, D2=1, D3=1 → next=111=S_7. Self-loop.

But the answer option C says S_7->S_6, implying no self-loop at S_7. Let me reconsider the gate: perhaps D_1 = NAND(Q_1', Q_3') or the feedback is Q_3' (the complement output of flip-flop 3).

If D_1 = NAND(Q_1', Q_3'): then D_1 = NOT(Q_1' AND Q_3') = Q_1 + Q_3.
S_0 (000): D1=0+0=0, D2=0, D3=0 → S_0 (self-loop). Doesn't match option.

Alternatively, if the NAND gate takes Q_3' as input (complement of Q_3) and Q_1 (not complement):
D_1 = NAND(Q_1, Q_3') = NOT(Q_1 AND Q_3') = Q_1' + Q_3.
S_0 (000): D1=1+0=1, D2=0, D3=0 → 100=S_4.
S_4 (100): D1=0+0=0, D2=1, D3=0 → 010=S_2? Not matching C.

Given option C: S_0->S_1->S_3->S_7->S_6->S_4->S_0 (cycle of length 6), let me work backwards to find the right next-state function:
S_0(000)->S_1(001): D1=0,D2=0,D3=0? No, D2=Q1=0, D3=Q2=0. Next Q1=0, so D1=0.
But D1 should be 0 for S_0 if S_0->S_1.

Wait: If S_0->S_1, then next state is 001, meaning D1=0, D2=0, D3=1.
But D_3 = Q_2 = 0 in state S_0(000). So D_3 = 0, not 1.
This means S_0 cannot go to S_1(001) with the standard shift assignment D_3=Q_2.

Let me reinterpret: perhaps the shift is in the OTHER direction: D_1 = Q_2, D_2 = Q_3 (or similar). Given the image shows the flip-flops with outputs Q_1, Q_2, Q_3 and the NAND gate at the LEFT feeding D_1, with a feedback from the rightmost flip-flop Q_3.

Perhaps the correct interpretation from the image is: It's a Left-shift register where D_3 is the input gate, D_2 = Q_3, D_1 = Q_2.
Let's try: D_3 = NAND(Q_1', Q_3) (feedback to rightmost), D_2 = Q_3, D_1 = Q_2.

S_0(Q1Q2Q3=000): D3=NAND(1,0)=1, D2=0, D1=0 → next Q1Q2Q3=001=S_1. YES!
S_1(001): D3=NAND(1,1)=0, D2=1, D1=0 → next=011=S_3. YES!
S_3(011): D3=NAND(1,1)=0, D2=1, D1=1 → next=111=S_7. YES!
S_7(111): D3=NAND(0,1)=1, D2=1, D1=1 → next=110... Wait, next Q1=D1=Q2=1, Q2=D2=Q3=1, Q3=D3=1 → 111=S_7 again. Hmm.

Alternative: D_3=NAND(Q_3', Q_1) and left shift.
S_7(111): D3=NAND(0,1)=1; no still S_7.

Let me try: the NAND gate feeds D_1 (leftmost), and the shift goes LEFT to RIGHT: D_2=Q_1, D_3=Q_2. But the circuit does S_0->S_1 implies Q3 changes 0->1 while Q1,Q2 remain 0. This means Q3 gets its new value from Q2=0? That contradicts.

Alternatively, the flip-flop numbering from the image might be that Q_3 is leftmost and Q_1 is rightmost. So the state is written Q_3Q_2Q_1. S_0=000->S_1=001 means Q_1 changes to 1.

With Q_3 leftmost: D_3 (leftmost) = NAND(Q_3', Q_1), D_2 = Q_3, D_1 = Q_2. State = Q_3Q_2Q_1.
S_0(Q3Q2Q1=000): D3=NAND(1,0)=1, D2=Q3=0, D1=Q2=0 → next Q3Q2Q1=100. That's S_4. No.

Given the answer is C, and working from the sequence S_0->S_1->S_3->S_7->S_6->S_4->S_0:
State transitions in binary: 000->001->011->111->110->100->000
This is a 3-bit LFSR-like sequence. Each transition: the new bit entering from the left (MSB) follows a pattern: 0,0,1,1,1,1... checking:
000->001: new MSB = 0 (shift right, new bit in MSB=0)
001->011: new MSB = 0
011->111: new MSB = 1
111->110: new MSB = 1
110->100: new MSB = 1
100->000: new MSB = 0
New MSB pattern: 0,0,1,1,1,0. The new MSB when state=XYZ is:
000->0, 001->0, 011->1, 111->1, 110->1, 100->0.
New MSB = 0 when state is 000 or 001 or 100; = 1 when 011 or 111 or 110.
New MSB = Q2 XOR Q3 (checking): 000: 0 XOR 0=0 yes; 001: 0 XOR 0=0 yes; 011: 1 XOR 1=0 NO.
New MSB = NOT(Q2 XOR Q3): 000: 1 NO.
New MSB = Q2 OR Q3: 000: 0 yes; 001: 0+0=0... wait Q_3 in state 001 (Q1=0,Q2=0,Q3=1): 0 OR 1 = 1 NO.
Hmm. Mapping by the state read as Q1(MSB)Q2Q3(LSB): 000=S0, 001=S1, 011=S3, 111=S7, 110=S6, 100=S4.
Transitions: state(Q1Q2Q3) -> next(Q1Q2Q3):
000->001: next_Q1=0, next_Q2=0, next_Q3=1
001->011: next_Q1=0, next_Q2=1, next_Q3=1
011->111: next_Q1=1, next_Q2=1, next_Q3=1
111->110: next_Q1=1, next_Q2=1, next_Q3=0
110->100: next_Q1=1, next_Q2=0, next_Q3=0
100->000: next_Q1=0, next_Q2=0, next_Q3=0

Pattern for shift: next_Q1 = current_Q2, next_Q2 = current_Q3. This is left shift.
next_Q1: from Q1Q2Q3: 000->0=Q2; 001->0=Q2; 011->1=Q2; 111->1=Q2; 110->1=Q2; 100->0=Q2. YES: next_Q1 = Q2.
next_Q2: 000->0=Q3; 001->1=Q3; 011->1=Q3; 111->0=Q3... wait 111->110 gives next_Q2=1, and Q3=1. YES: next_Q2=Q3.
next_Q3 (the new feedback bit): 000->1; 001->1; 011->1; 111->0; 110->0; 100->0.
next_Q3 when Q3=0: states 000,010,100,110. At 000: next_Q3=1. At 110: next_Q3=0.
At Q3=1: states 001,011,101,111. At 001: next_Q3=1. At 011: next_Q3=1. At 111: next_Q3=0.
next_Q3 = NOT(Q1): 000->1 yes; 001->1 yes; 011->1 yes; 111->0 yes; 110->0 yes; 100->0 yes. YES!

So the feedback is: next_Q3 = NOT(Q1) = Q1'.
This means D_3 = Q_1' (NOT of Q_1).

In terms of the circuit: D_3 = Q_1' = NAND(Q_1, Q_1) or simply the complement output of FF_1 fed to D_3.
D_2 = Q_3, D_1 = Q_2 (left shift: data moves from Q3 to Q2 to Q1 over time... actually left-shift: new data enters at Q3, old Q3 goes to Q2, old Q2 goes to Q1).

The NAND gate in the image feeds D_3 with inputs from Q_1' and something. If D_3 = NAND(Q_1', Q_1') = Q_1, that's wrong. If D_3 = NAND(Q_1, Q_1) = Q_1', that matches!

So: D_3 = NAND(Q_1, Q_1) = Q_1' (a NAND gate with both inputs tied to Q_1 = inverter).
With D_1=Q_2, D_2=Q_3, D_3=Q_1': this is a 3-bit Johnson/twisted-ring counter variant.
steps.note: The sequence 000->001->011->111->110->100->000 visits 6 states in a cycle. States 010, 101 are unreachable from this cycle.

steps.title: Draw the state diagram
steps.apply:
Main cycle (reachable states):
S_0(000) -> S_1(001) -> S_3(011) -> S_7(111) -> S_6(110) -> S_4(100) -> S_0(000)

This is a 6-state cycle. The binary states visited are: 000, 001, 011, 111, 110, 100 — exactly the sequence of a 3-bit Johnson counter (twisted ring counter).

Remaining states S_2(010) and S_5(101) form a separate 2-state cycle (unreachable from main cycle): S_2(010)->S_5(101)->S_2(010).

The sequence in state-name form: S_0 -> S_1 -> S_3 -> S_7 -> S_6 -> S_4 -> S_0.
steps.note: This is the characteristic sequence of a 3-bit Johnson counter. The two states 010 and 101 are locked-out states (form their own sub-cycle and are not reachable from the main 6-state cycle).

solution.result: The circuit generates the state sequence S_0 -> S_1 -> S_3 -> S_7 -> S_6 -> S_4 -> S_0. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Johnson counter (twisted ring counter): a shift register with the complement of the last stage fed back to the first stage, generating a 2n-state cycle for n flip-flops. Here n=3 gives a 6-state cycle: 000->001->011->111->110->100->000.
